summary:
The Haida of Haida Gwaii (Queen Charlotte Islands, British Columbia, c. pre-European contact through present) developed one of the most sophisticated Indigenous cultures in North America — a seafaring people whose highly stratified society, elaborate ceremonial culture, and extraordinary artistic tradition (particularly in wood carving and totem poles) made Haida Gwaii one of the cultural apexes of the Northwest Coast civilization complex. The Haida were renowned as skilled mariners whose war canoes (up to 70 feet long, carved from single cedar logs) ranged as far north as Alaska and as far south as California in raids for slaves and prestige goods; their social structure was organized around two moieties (Ravens and Eagles) and elaborate hereditary nobility marked by potlatch ceremonies in which wealth was publicly distributed.

The Haida artistic tradition is among North America's most celebrated: totem poles narrating clan histories, mortuary poles housing remains of chiefs, monumental house fronts, elaborately carved boxes, and argillite carvings (developed specifically for the European trade market after contact) achieved levels of formal sophistication recognized worldwide. European contact, beginning with Spanish and British explorers in the 1770s, brought devastating smallpox epidemics — the Haida population is estimated to have fallen from perhaps 15,000-20,000 in the 18th century to fewer than 600 by 1900 — one of the most catastrophic demographic collapses in North American history. European contact also brought trade goods that initially enhanced Haida artistic production (metal tools enabling finer carving) before the devastating disease population collapse disrupted the social fabric supporting artistic tradition. Haida cultural revitalization since the mid-20th century has restored much of this heritage.
